Unit 2 Project

Lecture 01

#Q1

penguins <- penguins %>%
  drop_na(flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, sex)
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = island)) +
  geom_bar()

The Biscoe Island has the most penguins.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = island)) +
  geom_bar() +
  theme_minimal()

The minimal theme changes the background of the histogram to be white instead of gray.

#Q2

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30) +
  theme_minimal()

The plot does not look normal. It looks slightly bimodal.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 10) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60) +
  theme_minimal()

Larger bin numbers make more descriptive but more cluttered plots while the smaller bin numbers make a cleaner plot, but lacks detail.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60) +
  facet_wrap(~ species) +
  theme_minimal()

The distribution shows that each of the species distributions are about unimodal, with Adelie penguins being the lightest on average and Gentoo penguins being the heaviest on average. Chinstrap penguins are in between the two. All the different species of penguin have different approximate means.

#Q3

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, y = flipper_length_mm)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  theme_minimal()

The Gentoo penguins have the longest median flipper length mm.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = sex, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  theme_minimal()

Yes, males and females differ significantly in body mass, though there is overlap due to different species being plotted together.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, y = flipper_length_mm, fill = species)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = sex, y = body_mass_g, fill = species)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  theme_minimal()

Yes, adding colors to the different species makes it more readable, though it is redundant because the plot already displays the different species. However, adding the fill species to the second plot created makes a very readable plot that separated body mass g by sex and then species after that.

#Q4

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal()

The relationship looks unclear looking at the data from the scatter plot.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color = species)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal()

By adding the color coding, the dots create clusters by species, so it did change my interpretation.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color = species)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  theme_minimal()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  theme_minimal()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

The line does fit the data well on the plot that separates the data points by species with color, but overall, without the color signifying different species, the line looks more random.

#Q5

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~ species) +
  theme_minimal()

The Gentoo species shows the strongest positive relationship between body mass and flipper length.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_grid(sex ~ species) +
  theme_minimal()

Across all the species, males typically weigh more than the females, with Gentoo penguins being the heaviest species overall. There is a positive relationship shown between body mass and flipper length across all species.

#Q6

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, y = body_mass_g, fill = sex)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  facet_wrap(~ species) +
  labs(
    title = "Body Mass by Species and Sex",
    x = "Species",
    y = "Body Mass (g)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

I created a plot showing body mass across penguin species, separated and color coded by sex.

#Q7

I had the most fun messing with the color coding for the plots, and seeing how I could use the color coding to further organize my plot. The most difficult part of this exercise was learning the new commands and making sure I didn’t make any errors in my code.

Lecture 02

penguins_messy <- read.csv("penguins_messy.csv")

#Q1

str_detect(penguins_messy$species_messy, "adelie")
##   [1]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [13]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE

str_replace_all(penguins_messy$sex_messy, "\\?", "")

## [253] "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"  
## [259] "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"  
## [265] "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"  
## [271] "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"   "BIS-COE"  
## [277] "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"    
## [283] "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"    
## [289] "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"    
## [295] "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"    
## [301] "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"    
## [307] "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"    
## [313] "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"    
## [319] "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"    
## [325] "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"    
## [331] "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"    
## [337] "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"    
## [343] "Dream"     "Dream"

The first command returns true or false depending on if the species_messy value has the text Adelie. The second command removes all the “?” characters in the sex_messy values. The third command collapses the spaces in between the text in island_messy to make it look nice.

#Q2

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(valid_id = str_detect(sample_id, "^PAL-[0-9]{3}$"))

penguins_messy %>% pull(valid_id) %>% sum()
## [1] 316

There are 316 rows that have valid ids

#Q3

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  filter(valid_id == TRUE)

#Q4

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(
    file_date = str_extract(file_name, "[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}"),
    file_id   = str_extract(file_name, "PAL-[0-9]{3}")
  )

head(penguins_messy$file_date, 10)
##  [1] "2024-01-14" "2024-01-17" "2024-01-08" "2024-01-26" "2024-01-06"
##  [6] "2024-01-08" "2024-01-08" "2024-01-19" "2024-01-14" "2024-01-24"
head(penguins_messy$file_id, 10)
##  [1] "PAL-535" "PAL-442" "PAL-668" "PAL-920" "PAL-223" "PAL-899" "PAL-268"
##  [8] "PAL-139" "PAL-847" "PAL-238"

#Q5

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(island = str_squish(island_messy))

unique(penguins_messy$island)
## [1] "Torgersen" "BIS-COE"   "Dream"

#Q6

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(sex = str_to_upper(str_replace_all(sex_messy, "\\?", "")))

unique(penguins_messy$sex)
## [1] "M" "F" NA

#Q7

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(
    bill_mm = notes %>%
      str_replace_all("(.*bill=)|mm", "") %>%
      as.numeric()
  )
## Warning: There was 1 warning in `mutate()`.
## ℹ In argument: `bill_mm = notes %>% str_replace_all("(.*bill=)|mm", "") %>%
##   as.numeric()`.
## Caused by warning in `notes %>% str_replace_all("(.*bill=)|mm", "") %>% as.numeric()`:
## ! NAs introduced by coercion
head(penguins_messy$bill_mm, 10)
##  [1] 39.1 39.5 40.3   NA 36.7 39.3 38.9 39.2 34.1 42.0

#Q8

The most interesting part of the exercise was trying to use the normal expressions to clean the data. The most confusing part of the exercise was trying to figure out why some values returned N/A. I have a pretty good idea on how to use regular expressions now.

Lecture 03

#Q1

penguins_clean <- penguins %>%
  drop_na(bill_length_mm, sex)

nrow(penguins)
## [1] 333
nrow(penguins_clean)
## [1] 333

The “penguins” dataset has 344 rows and penguins_clean has 333

#Q2

penguins_dup <- penguins_clean %>%
  bind_rows(slice_sample(penguins_clean, n = 30, replace = TRUE))

nrow(penguins_dup)
## [1] 363
nrow(distinct(penguins_dup))
## [1] 333

The penguins_dup dataset has 363 rows, after removing the exact duplicates using distinct(), there are 333 rows. Duplicates can mess up summary statistics and plots because they can inflate the sample size, and thereby bias averages and proportions, ultimately impacting the overall trend.

#Q3

penguins_q3 <- penguins_clean %>%
  select(species, island, sex, where(is.numeric))

penguins_q3
## # A tibble: 333 × 8
##    species island    sex    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm
##    <fct>   <fct>     <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>
##  1 Adelie  Torgersen male             39.1          18.7               181
##  2 Adelie  Torgersen female           39.5          17.4               186
##  3 Adelie  Torgersen female           40.3          18                 195
##  4 Adelie  Torgersen female           36.7          19.3               193
##  5 Adelie  Torgersen male             39.3          20.6               190
##  6 Adelie  Torgersen female           38.9          17.8               181
##  7 Adelie  Torgersen male             39.2          19.6               195
##  8 Adelie  Torgersen female           41.1          17.6               182
##  9 Adelie  Torgersen male             38.6          21.2               191
## 10 Adelie  Torgersen male             34.6          21.1               198
## # ℹ 323 more rows
## # ℹ 2 more variables: body_mass_g <int>, year <int>
ncol(penguins_q3)
## [1] 8

There are 8 columns in the resulting dataframe.

#Q4

penguins_clean %>%
  select(starts_with("bill"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 2
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>
##  1           39.1          18.7
##  2           39.5          17.4
##  3           40.3          18  
##  4           36.7          19.3
##  5           39.3          20.6
##  6           38.9          17.8
##  7           39.2          19.6
##  8           41.1          17.6
##  9           38.6          21.2
## 10           34.6          21.1
## # ℹ 323 more rows
penguins_clean %>%
  select(ends_with("_mm"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 3
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>
##  1           39.1          18.7               181
##  2           39.5          17.4               186
##  3           40.3          18                 195
##  4           36.7          19.3               193
##  5           39.3          20.6               190
##  6           38.9          17.8               181
##  7           39.2          19.6               195
##  8           41.1          17.6               182
##  9           38.6          21.2               191
## 10           34.6          21.1               198
## # ℹ 323 more rows
penguins_clean %>%
  select(matches("bill|flipper"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 3
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>
##  1           39.1          18.7               181
##  2           39.5          17.4               186
##  3           40.3          18                 195
##  4           36.7          19.3               193
##  5           39.3          20.6               190
##  6           38.9          17.8               181
##  7           39.2          19.6               195
##  8           41.1          17.6               182
##  9           38.6          21.2               191
## 10           34.6          21.1               198
## # ℹ 323 more rows

#Q5

penguins_clean %>%
  group_by(species) %>%
  summarize(across(where(is.numeric), mean))
## # A tibble: 3 × 6
##   species   bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g  year
##   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <dbl>       <dbl> <dbl>
## 1 Adelie              38.8          18.3              190.       3706. 2008.
## 2 Chinstrap           48.8          18.4              196.       3733. 2008.
## 3 Gentoo              47.6          15.0              217.       5092. 2008.

Using across(where(is.numeric), …) is better because it automatically applies the function to all numeric columns without having listing them all out.

#Q6

penguins_clean <- penguins_clean %>%
  mutate(
    bill_length = case_when(
      bill_length_mm < 40 ~ "short",
      bill_length_mm < 50 ~ "medium",
      bill_length_mm >= 50 ~ "long"
    ))

#Q7

instrument_meta <- tibble(
  instrument_id  = c("caliper_A", "caliper_B", "caliper_C"),
  calibration_mm = c(0.2, 0.5, 1.0),
  manufacturer   = c("Mitutoyo", "Fowler", "Generic")
)

penguins_instrumented <- penguins_clean %>%
  drop_na(bill_length_mm) %>%
  mutate(
    instrument_id = case_when(
      year == 2007 ~ "caliper_A",
      year == 2008 ~ "caliper_B",
      year == 2009 ~ "caliper_C"
    )) %>%
  left_join(instrument_meta, by = "instrument_id")

ncol(penguins_clean)
## [1] 9
ncol(penguins_instrumented)
## [1] 12

The number of columns in penguins_instrumented is 12 and penguins_clean has 9.

#Q8

The most interesting part of the exercise was seeing the effects that duplication has on the plots and analyses. The hardest part was trying to understand the logic behind left_join(). Yes the tips would help me clean the data faster in the future.
